coredata.dc:title: Neuromodulatory properties of fluorescent carbon dots: Effect on exocytotic release, uptake and ambient level of glutamate and GABA in brain nerve terminals
coredata.prism:publicationName: The International Journal of Biochemistry & Cell Biology
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Borisova, Tatiana
Nazarova, Anastasia
Dekaliuk, Mariia
Krisanova, Natalia
Pozdnyakova, Natalia
Borysov, Arsenii
Sivko, Roman
Demchenko, Alexander P.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots), a recently discovered class of fluorescent nano-sized particles with pure carbon core, have great bioanalytical potential. Neuroactive properties of fluorescent C-dots obtained from β-alanine by microwave heating were assessed based on the analysis of their effects on the key characteristics of GABA- and glutamatergic neurotransmission in isolated rat brain nerve terminals. It was found that C-dots (40–800μg/ml) in dose-dependent manner: (1) decreased exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate; (2) reduced acidification of synaptic vesicles; (3) attenuated the initial velocity of Na+-dependent transporter-mediated uptake of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate; (4) increased the ambient level of the neurotransmitters, nevertheless (5) did not change significantly the potential of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals. Almost complete suppression of exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters was caused by C-dots at a concentration of 800μg/ml. Fluorescent and neuromodulatory features combined in C-dots create base for their potential usage for labeling and visualization of key processes in nerve terminals, and also in theranostics. In addition, natural presence of carbon-containing nanoparticles in the human food chain and in the air may provoke the development of neurologic consequences.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Neurotransmitters
Exocytosis
Na+-dependent uptake
Brain nerve terminals

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: The International Journal of Biochemistry & Cell Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Isolation of rat brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes)
Analysis of the interaction of C-dots with rat brain nerve terminals
Measurement of synaptosomal plasma membrane potential (E m)
Measurements of synaptic vesicle acidification in the synaptosomes
Analysis of the size of synaptosomes in the presence of C-dots by dynamic light scattering
Fluorescence imaging of synaptosomes labeled with C-dots
Effects of С-dots on Са2+-dependent release of neurotransmitters (exocytosis) from nerve terminals stimulated by depolarization of the plasma membrane
Effects of C-dots on exocytotic release of [3H]GABA from nerve terminals
Effects of C-dots on exocytotic release of l-[14C]glutamate from nerve terminals
Effects of С-dots on acidification of synaptic vesicles
Membrane potential of nerve terminals in the presence of С-dots
Influence of C-dots on the functioning of high-affinity Na+-dependent neurotransmitter transporters in the plasma membrane
Influence of C-dots on high-affinity transporter-mediated [3H]GABA uptake
Influence of C-dots on high-affinity transporter-mediated l-[14C]glutamate uptake
Influence of С-dots on the ambient level of the neurotransmitters in nerve terminals
Effects of С-dots on the ambient level of [3Н]САВА
Effects of C-dots on the ambient level of l-[14C]glutamate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Light absorption (A) and fluoresence excitation and emission (B) spectra of the C-dots used in this study. Excitation wavelengths 315nm (blue) and 350nm (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The change of fluorescence intensity of C-dots on the variation of pH (A) and in solvents of different polarity (B).

The change in fluorescence spectra after incubation of synaptosomes with С-dots for 10, 30, 60min and subsequent washing out of unreacted C-dots. The spectra were recorded at the maximum of excitation of C-dots (350nm). Control—autofluorescence of synaptosomes.

Dynamic light scattering histograms of synaptosomal suspension (0.2mg/ml) in a standard saline solution in the absence (A) and presence (B) of C-dots (400μg/ml). Three measurements each during 1min, sequentially 1—blue, 2—black, 3—red, are present in each histogram. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of synaptosomes labeled by C-dots (A) and lipid order sensitive biomembrane dye NR12S (B). Scale bar is 10μm. Synaptosomes preloaded with C-dots are visualized as bright round spots clustered in groups.

Stimulated by 35mM KCl exocytotic release of [3H]GABA (A) and l-[14C]glutamate (B) from synaptosomes in control experiment (1) and in the presence of С-dots at increasing concentrations of 40μg/ml (2); 80μg/ml (3); 200μg/ml (4); 400μg/ml (5); 800μg/ml (6). Data is mean±SEM of three independent experiments, each of them was performed with different synaptosomal preparations in triplicate. * p <0.05; ** p <0.01; *** p <0.001 as compared to control.

Acidification of synaptosomes in the presence of С-dots. (A)—The synaptosomes were equilibrated with acridine orange (5μM); when the steady level of the dye fluorescence had been reached, the С-dots at a concentration of 50μg/ml (one arrow) were added. (B)—Acridine orange fluorescence after the addition of C-dots (without synaptosomes). Trace represents four experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations.

(A) Membrane potential of synaptosomes after the addition of С-dots. The suspension of synaptosomes was equilibrated with potential-sensitive dye rhodamine 6G (0.5μM); when the steady level of the dye fluorescence had been reached, the С-dots at a concentration of 50μg/ml (marked by arrow) were added to synaptosomes. (B) Rhodamine 6G fluorescence after the addition of C-dots (without synaptosomes). Trace represents four experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations.

[3H]GABA (A) and l-[14C]glutamate (B) uptake by synaptosomes in control (1) and in the presence of C-dots at a concentration of 40μg/ml (2); 80μg/ml (3); 200μg/ml (4); 400μg/ml (5) and 800μg/ml (6). Data is mean±SEM of three independent experiments, each of them was performed with different synaptosomal preparations in triplicate.

The extracellular level of [3H]GABA (A) and l-[14C]glutamate (B) in synaptosomes in control (1) and in the presence of С-dots at а concentration of 40μg/ml (2); 80μg/ml (3); 200μg/ml (4); 400μg/ml (5); and 800μg/ml (6). Data is mean±SEM of three independent experiments, each of them was performed with different synaptosomal preparations in triplicate. * p <0.05; ** p <0.001 as compared to control.

head.ce:title: Neuromodulatory properties of fluorescent carbon dots: Effect on exocytotic release, uptake and ambient level of glutamate and GABA in brain nerve terminals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept Neurochemistry and Lab. of Nanobiotechnology, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine, 9 Leontovicha str, Kiev, Ukraine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dept Neurochemistry and Lab. of Nanobiotechnology, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots), a recently discovered class of fluorescent nano-sized particles with pure carbon core, have great bioanalytical potential. Neuroactive properties of fluorescent C-dots obtained from β-alanine by microwave heating were assessed based on the analysis of their effects on the key characteristics of GABA- and glutamatergic neurotransmission in isolated rat brain nerve terminals. It was found that C-dots (40–800μg/ml) in dose-dependent manner: (1) decreased exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate; (2) reduced acidification of synaptic vesicles; (3) attenuated the initial velocity of Na+-dependent transporter-mediated uptake of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate; (4) increased the ambient level of the neurotransmitters, nevertheless (5) did not change significantly the potential of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals. Almost complete suppression of exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters was caused by C-dots at a concentration of 800μg/ml. Fluorescent and neuromodulatory features combined in C-dots create base for their potential usage for labeling and visualization of key processes in nerve terminals, and also in theranostics. In addition, natural presence of carbon-containing nanoparticles in the human food chain and in the air may provoke the development of neurologic consequences.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, an increased attention is focused on the investigation of new unusual properties of inorganic carbon materials that can be implemented in nanoscale devices and biotechnologies. Carbon dots (C-dots) comprise a recently discovered class of strongly fluorescent, emission-color-tuning and non-blinking nanoparticles with great analytical and bioanalytical potential (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Li et al., 2012). There are many methods of obtaining them, the most popular of which is the microwave-assisted pyrolysis (Zhai et al., 2012) of organic matter (Chandra et al., 2012) and even of waste natural products, such as coffee grounds (Hsu et al., 2012) and soy milk (Zhou et al., 2012). C-dots can be viewed as a highly defected composition of coexisting aromatic and aliphatic regions, the elementary constituents of which are graphene, graphene oxide and diamond. They are assembled in proportions and with the variations of surface groups that depend on the original material and the conditions of their synthesis. Regarding optical properties, the absorption and emission spectra of C-dots, obtained by various methods, are remarkably similar and strongly resemble that observed for other species of nanocarbon family, graphene and graphene oxide nanoparticles (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013). Their fluorescence emission is typically concentrated in the blue and green ranges of spectra and the positions of their band maxima often depend on the wavelength of excitation, suggesting the evidence for the presence of multiple fluorophores. C-dots exhibit nanosecond lifetimes and high two-photonic cross-sections (Baker and Baker, 2010). Their quantum yields were already reported on a relatively high level of 30–40% (Dong et al., 2012; Zhai et al., 2012).

It is clear that bioimaging sensors for in vivo bioanalytical diagnostics must be non-toxic and biocompatible. In contrast to semiconductor nanomaterials (quantum dots), which usually contain cadmium, the C-dots comprise non-toxic elements, which makes them promising bioanalytical tools (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011). It has been demonstrated that the C-dots have a very low cytotoxicity and they can be internalized to the cells probably by endocytosis (Bhunia et al., 2013; Luo et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2011). The translocation of C-dots through cellular membrane was found to be temperature dependent and no internalization was observed at +4°C. Inside the cells, the C-dots are accumulated preferentially in the membrane and the cytoplasm (Cao et al., 2007; Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Sun et al., 2006).

The uniqueness of the C-dots as fluorescent agents and their prospective usage as possible fluorescent markers for biological objects requires detailed analysis of not only their toxic properties in standard tests but also their possible effects on different cellular processes. Moreover, the physiological health-related effects of C-dots may be of interest not only from nanobiotechnological point of view. They were detected in different carbohydrate-based food caramels, namely bread, sugar caramel, corn flakes and biscuits, where the preparation involves heating of the original material (Kenneth, 2013; Palashuddin Sk et al., 2012). Also, carbon-containing nanoparticles originated from burning of carbohydrate-based products may be present in air pollution. The facts that carbon nanoparticles are minor nevertheless ubiquitous components of human food chain and the air require considering their health and environmental risks and make this problem multidisciplinary involving medicine, ecology, environmental and nutrition science.

Glutamate and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) are key excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters in the mammalian central nervous system (Danbolt, 2001). They are implicated in many aspects of normal brain functioning. Abnormal glutamate and GABA homeostasis contributes to neuronal dysfunction and it is involved in the pathogenesis of major neurological disorders. Exocytosis, the fusion of synaptic vesicles containing neurotransmitter with plasma membrane, is the main mechanism of the release of neurotransmitters from presynaptic nerve terminals. Under normal physiological conditions, extracellular GABA and glutamate between episodes of exocytotic release is maintained at a low level preventing continual activation of receptors of neurotransmitters (Borisova et al., 2010a). The Na+-coupled neurotransmitter transporters are key players in the termination of synaptic neurotransmission and mediate uptake of amino acid neurotransmitters into the cytosol. Glutamate and GABA transporters belong to the different families. Essentially, the glutamate transporters belong to the SLC1 family, whereas GABA transporters (as well as carriers for the biogenic monoamines and glycine) belong to the SLC6 family. The transporters use Na+/K+ electrochemical gradients across the plasma membrane as a driving force. Then, neurotransmitters are accumulated in synaptic vesicles, which are the acidic compartments of nerve terminals, which store neurotransmitters and release their contents by exocytosis upon stimulation. Active transport of glutamate, acetylcholine, monoamines, γ-aminobutyric acid/glycine to synaptic vesicles is mediated by special vesicular transporters of the neurotransmitters, which depends on the proton electrochemical gradient ΔμH+ generated by V-ATPase that electrogenically pumps protons into the vesicle interior. It is clear that the lower the proton gradient in synaptic vesicles, the less the accumulation of the neurotransmitters in synaptic vesicles, and also their exocytotic release will be. Dissipation of the proton gradient by bafilomycin A1, a highly specific inhibitor of V-type ATPase, or by the protonophore FCCP causes a decrease in KCl-evoked exocytotic release of glutamate and in the initial velocity of glutamate uptake by synaptosomes (Borisova et al., 2011b, Borisova and Krisanova, 2008).

The aim of the present study was to assess neuroactive properties of C-dots analyzing their effects on key characteristics of GABA- and glutamatergic neurotransmission in presynaptic rat brain nerve terminals. The following parameters were studied: (1) exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters using radiolabeled [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate; (2) acidification of synaptic vesicles using pH-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange; (3) the membrane potential (E m) of the plasma membrane using potential-sensitive fluorescent dye rhodamine 6G; (4) uptake of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate via specific high-affinity Na+-dependent plasma membrane transporters; (5) the ambient level of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate.

The studied carbon nanoparticles (C-dots) were prepared from β-alanine by the method of Jiang et al. (2012) that was primarily applied for the microwave treatment of histidine. Specifically: 0.5g of β-alanine was dissolved in 2ml of distilled water in open vessel and treated in microwave oven (600W, 1.5min). The obtained material was diluted with distilled water and followed by repeated centrifugation (10min×5000× g) with the collection of supernatant. To obtain an accurate concentration of the particles, the supernatant was dried to the attainment of constant weight. Thereafter, 7mg/ml concentration was prepared by dissolving the product in distilled water to conduct further research. According to literature data the structure of water-soluble C-dots is characterized by inorganic carbon core in the form of sp2 hybridized graphene islands with the inclusion of diamond-like sp3 hybridized elements. The shell is represented by amorphous carbon and oxygen-containing polar groups (carboxyls, carbonyls and hydroxyls). In our case the nitrogen-containing groups are also presented at the surface in an NH2 state and also as aromatic amines included into pyrole rings (Wei et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2012). In additional experiments, synthesized C-dots were purified by column chromatography using Sephadex G-100 to exclude the presence of unreacted precursors. Purified C-dots demonstrated similar effects on the key characteristics of GABA- and glutamatergic neurotransmission as compared to those before column chromatography.

The obtained C-dots were controlled by recording the absorption spectra (spectrophotometer Perkin Elmer Lambda-bio). Their excitation and emission fluorescence spectra were obtained using spectrofluorimeter QuantaMaster 40 (PTI, Canada).
Fluorescence quantum yield φ of synthesized C-dots was calculated by conventional method using quinine sulfate solution as the reference with φ =0.55 (Fletcher, 1969).

Wistar male rats, 100–120g body weight, were obtained from the vivarium of M.D. Strazhesko Institute of Cardiology, Medical Academy of Sciences of Ukraine. Animals were kept in animal facilities of the Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, housed in a quiet, temperature-controlled room (22–23°C) and were provided with water and dry food pellets ad libitum. All procedures were conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki (“Scientific Requirements and Research Protocols” and “Research Ethics Committees”). Experimental protocols were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of the Palladin Institute of Biochemistry (Protocol from 19/09-2011). Before removing the brain, rats were sacrificed by rapid decapitation (Hensler et al., 2012). The total number of animals used in the study was 32, i.e. the assessment of glutamate uptake, release, the extracellular level—9 animals (3 animals per parameter); GABA uptake, release, the extracellular level—9 animals (3 animals per parameter); fluorescent measurements—13 animals (membrane potential—4 animals, acidification of synaptic vesicles—4 animals, dynamic light scattering, C-dots characteristics and microscopy—5 animals).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of rat brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes)
ce:section.ce:para: Cerebral hemispheres of decapitated animals were rapidly removed and homogenized in ice-cold 0.32M sucrose, 5mM HEPES-NaOH, pH 7.4, and 0.2mM EDTA. One animal was used to obtain one synaptosomal preparation, and each measurement was performed in triplicate. The synaptosomes were prepared by differential and Ficoll-400 density gradient centrifugation of rat brain homogenate according to the method of Cotman (1974) with slight modifications (Krisanova et al., 2012). All manipulations were performed at 4°C. The synaptosomal suspensions were used in experiments during 2–4h after isolation. The standard saline solution was oxygenated and contained (in mM): NaCl 126; KCl 5; MgCl2 2.0; NaH2PO4 1.0; HEPES 20, pH 7.4; and d-glucose 10. Ca2+-supplemented medium contained 2mM CaCl2. The Ca2+-free medium contained 1mM EGTA and no added Ca2+. Protein concentration was measured as described by Larson et al. (1986).

Analysis of the size of synaptosomes in the presence of C-dots was performed by dynamic light scattering using a laser correlation spectrometer “ZetaSizer-3” (Malvern Instrument, UK), equipped with He–Ne laser LGN-111 (p =25mW, λ =633nm) at scattering angle 90°. The dynamic range of this instrument is from 1nm to 50μm. Suspension (1ml) was placed in a thermostated cylindrical quartz cuvette, 10mm in diameter. Registration and statistical processing of light scattered from aqueous (n =1.33) suspensions of C-dots were performed repeatedly for 60s at +22°С. The laser correlation spectrometer was equipped with multi-computing correlator type 7032 ce. The results of measurements were processed using computer software service PCS-Size mode v1.61.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the interaction of C-dots with rat brain nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots (2.8μg/ml of final concentration) were suspended in a standard saline solution, then synaptosomes (0.2mg/ml of final protein concentration) were added and the suspension was incubated at 37°C for 30min. The samples were collected, rapidly sedimented in a microcentrifuge (20s at 10,000× g), then the pellets were washed twice by 10 volume of a standard saline solution and centrifuged to remove excess of the dots. After that, the pellets were resuspended in a standard saline solution.

Fluorescence images were acquired on confocal laser scanning microscope LSM 510META, Carl Zeiss, with objective Plan-Apochromat 100×/1,4 Oil DIC. Synaptosomes (0.2mg of protein/ml) were incubated with C-dots (2.8μg/ml of final concentration) in a standard saline solution at 37°C for 30min, and thereafter washed twice by 10 volume of a standard saline solution and centrifuged (7200× g ×10min) to remove excess of the dots. The pellet was resuspended in a standard saline solution. The 365nm band was used for excitation with xenon lamp and the emitted light was collected using a 505nm filter. The experiments were performed at room temperature. Also, membrane dye NR12S was used to verify the integrity of the synaptosomal membrane. It was added to synaptosomes (0.2mg of protein/ml) at a final concentration 9nM for 10min. Due to high affinity of NR12S to membranes and its very low fluorescence in water, acquired images were obtained without washing out of the dye.

The synaptosomes were diluted in standard saline solution to 2mg of protein/ml and after pre-incubation for 10min at 37°C were loaded with [3H]GABA (50nM, 4.7μCi/ml) in the oxygenated standard saline solution for 10min. 100μM aminooxyacetic acid was present throughout all experiments of [3H]GABA loading and release. After loading, the suspension was washed with 10 volumes of ice-cold oxygenated standard saline solution. The pellet was re-suspended in a standard saline solution containing no Ca2+ to obtain protein concentration of 1mg of protein/ml and divided into two parts. 2mM CaCl2 was added to one part (Ca2+-supplemented media), 1mM EGTA—to other part (Ca2+-free media). Synaptosomes (120μl of the suspension) in both media were preincubated for 2min with C-dots (40–800μg/ml), and then [3H]GABA release was initiated by depolarization of synaptosomes with 35mM KCl. Samples were incubated for 6min at 37°C and then rapidly sedimented in a microcentrifuge (10,000× g, 20s). [3H]GABA was measured in the aliquots of supernatants (90μl) by liquid scintillation counting with scintillation cocktail ACS (1.5ml) and expressed as percentage of a total [3H]GABA accumulated.

In Ca2+-containing media, depolarization of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals by high KCl level induced release of neurotransmitters from both pools, i.e., vesicular and cytosolic. In Ca2+-free media, high-KCl induced neurotransmitter transporter reversal, and so transporter-mediated release of neurotransmitters from cytosolic pool. So, net Ca2+-dependent exocytotic release of [3H]GABA was calculated by subtraction of the value of neurotransmitter release in Ca2+-free media from that in Ca2+-containing media. Data is mean±SEM of three independent experiments each performed with different synaptosomal preparations in triplicate.

Neurotransmitter release from the synaptosomes incubated for different time intervals (0min and 6min) without stimulating agents was used for assay of unstimulated (basal) release, and expressed in pmol of [3H]GABA/mg of protein.

Synaptosomes were diluted in standard saline solution to reach concentration of 2mg of protein/ml and after pre-incubation at 37°C for 10min they were loaded with l-[14C]glutamate (1nmol/mg of protein, 238mCi/mmol) in Ca2+-supplemented oxygenated standard saline solution at 37°C for 10min. After loading, suspension was washed with 10 volumes of ice-cold oxygenated standard saline solution; the pellet was re-suspended in a solution to a final concentration of 1mg protein/ml and immediately used for release experiments. Release of l-[14C]glutamate from synaptosomes was performed in both Ca2+-containing and Ca2+-free incubation media according to the following method. Synaptosomal suspension (125μl; 0.5mg of protein/ml) was pre-incubated for 10min, then the C-dots (40–800μg/ml) were added at 37°C and incubated for different time intervals (0min and 6min) and rapidly sedimented using a microcentrifuge (20s at 10,000× g). Release was measured in the aliquots of the supernatants (100μl) and pellets by liquid scintillation counting with scintillation cocktail ACS (1.5ml). The result was expressed a percentage of total amount of radiolabeled neurotransmitter incorporated (Borisova, 2013). Net Ca2+-dependent exocytotic release of l-[14C]glutamate was calculated by subtraction of the value of neurotransmitter release in Ca2+-free media from that in Ca2+-containing media.

Release of the neurotransmitter from synaptosomes incubated for different time intervals (0min and 6min) without stimulating agents was used for assay of unstimulated release. Data collected in triplicate in three independent experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations each are presented as mean±SEM.

Synaptosomes were diluted in standard saline solution containing GABA transaminase inhibitor aminooxiacetic acid (100μM) to minimize formation of GABA metabolites. Concentration of protein in synaptosomal samples was 200μg/ml. Samples were preincubated at 37°C for 8min, then C-dots (40–800μg/ml) were added to the synaptosomal suspension and incubated for 2min. Uptake was initiated by the addition of GABA and [3H]GABA (1μM and 50nM–0.1μCi/ml, respectively). GABA uptake was terminated in different time intervals (1, 3, 5min) by filtering aliquots through a Whatman GF/C filters. After twice washing with 5ml ice-cold standard saline, filters were dried, then were suspended in Organic Counting Scintillant and counted in a Delta 300 (Tracor Analytic, USA) scintillation counter (Pozdnyakova et al., 2011). Non-specific binding of the neurotransmitter was evaluated in cooling samples sedimented immediately after the addition of radiolabeled GABA. Each measurement was performed in triplicate.

Uptake of l-[14C]glutamate by synaptosomes was measured as follows. Synaptosomal suspension (125μl; 0.2mg of protein/ml) was pre-incubated in standard saline solution at 37°C for 8min, then C-dots (40–800μg/ml) were added to the synaptosomal suspension and incubated for 2min. Uptake was initiated by the addition of 10μM l-glutamate supplemented with 420nM l-[14C]glutamate (0.1μCi/ml), incubated at 37°C during different time intervals (1, 2, 10min) and then rapidly sedimented using a microcentrifuge (20s at 10,000× g). l-[14C]glutamate uptake was determined as a decrease in radioactivity in aliquots of the supernatant (100μl) and an increase in radioactivity of the pellet (SDS-treated) measured by liquid scintillation counting with ACS scintillation cocktail (1.5ml) (Krisanova et al., 2013). Data collected in triplicate in three independent experiments performed with different synaptosomal preparations each are presented as mean±SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of synaptosomal plasma membrane potential (E m)
ce:section.ce:para:
Membrane potential was measured using a potentiometric fluorescent dye rhodamine 6G (0.5μM) based on its potential-modulated binding to the plasma membrane (Kasatkina and Borisova, 2010). The suspension of synaptosomes (0.2mg/ml of final protein concentration) after preincubation at 37°C for 10min was added to stirred thermostated cuvette. To estimate changes in the plasma membrane potential the ratio (F) as an index of membrane potential was calculated according to the following equation:
F =
F t
F 0
where F 0 and F
t are fluorescence intensities of a fluorescent dye in the absence and presence of the synaptosomes, respectively. F 0 was calculated by extrapolation of exponential decay function to t =0.
Fluorescence measurements with Rhodamine 6G were carried using a Hitachi MPF-4 spectrofluorimeter at 528nm (excitation) and 551nm (emission) wavelengths (slit bands 5nm each).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of synaptic vesicle acidification in the synaptosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Acridine orange, a pH-sensitive fluorescent dye, is known to be selectively accumulated by the acid compartments of synaptosomes (synaptic vesicles) (Borisova et al., 2011a). Therefore it was used for monitoring synaptic vesicle acidification. Fluorescence changes were measured using a Hitachi MPF-4 spectrofluorimeter at excitation and emission wavelengths of 490 and 530nm, respectively (slit bands 5nm each). Reaction was started by the addition of acridine orange (final concentration 5μM) to synaptosomal suspension (0.2mg/ml of final protein concentration) preincubated in a stirred thermostated cuvette at 30°C for 10min. The equilibrium level of dye fluorescence was achieved after 3min. Fluorescence (F) was determined according to Eq. (1).
Results were expressed as mean±S.E.M. of n independent experiments. Statistical difference within and between groups was verified by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

EGTA, EDTA, HEPES, β-alanine were purchased from Sigma (U.S.A.). Acridine orange and rhodamine 6G were obtained from Molecular Probes (U.S.A). Ficoll 400, L-[14C]glutamate, aqueous counting scintillant (ACS) were from Amersham (UK). Analytical grade salts were from Sigma (U.S.A.). Fluorescence dye NR12S labeling specifically biological membranes was a gift of Dr. Andrey Klymchenko (University of Strasbourg, France).

The light absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic properties of studied C-dots are typical to those described in the literature (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013). Their characteristic features are very strong light absorbance in the far UV region and dramatic decrease of absorbance in the near-UV, at the wavelengths of excitation band maximum (Fig. 1A). Excitation spectrum is also characteristic (Fig. 1B). In contrast to semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescence is not excited in the far UV region, so that the excitation spectrum usually represents a narrow band with the maximum in the region around 350nm. In our particular case the spectrum demonstrates heterogeneity of emitters that can be seen as the presence of two closely located bands at 315 and 350nm in excitation and the spectral shifts in emission bands when fluorescence is excited at these band maxima. Meantime, this type of heterogeneity is not as significant as in many other reported cases (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013; Luo et al., 2013). The violet fluorescence of these particles is not typical, since in most of the cases fluorescence is observed shifted to blue and blue–green regions. These properties depend on the original material and the composition of polar groups on the particle surface. In all studied cases the core of C-dots is formed by inorganic carbon in the form of islands of graphene-like heterocycles with the diamond-type inclusions (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011). The shells are formed by oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyls, carbonyls and carboxyls) that allow the particles to be soluble in water. Using amino acids as the original material results in inclusion of nitrogen-containing groups in the form of primary amines and aromatic pyroles into the polar shell, and this inclusion increases the quantum yield of fluorescence and shifts the fluorescence spectrum to shorter wavelengths (Philippidis et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2014). Our spectroscopic data are in line with these results. Though the surface engineering of C-dots has become a field of active research (Ding et al., 2014), in the present study no attempt of its modification have been made.

The fluorescence quantum yield φ of C-dots was found to be 28%, which is an indicator of their high brightness. It should be noted that the absorption band that corresponds to emissive state is unresolved and may be hidden under the tail of strong UV band (see Fig. 1A). Thus, the true fluorophore absorbance must be lower, which suggests that the φ value is underestimated.

The results of experiments shown in Fig. 2 demonstrate that fluorescence intensity of C-dots used in this study does not change significantly with the variation of pH in aqueous milieu and polarity of the solvent medium. With variation of solvent no spectral shifts was observed (the data not shown). Due to this stability of spectroscopic parameters it can be expected that no significant changes of fluorescence signal should be observed on the inclusion of C-dots into biological systems, where the local interactions of these nanoparticles cannot be estimated easily.

For the assessment of fluorescence properties of C-dots in the presence of isolated nerve terminals (synaptosomes), the latest (0.2mg/ml of final protein concentration) were incubated with C-dots at a concentration of 2.8μg/ml and washed twice by 10-fold volume of a standard saline solution as described in Experimental Section. As shown in Fig. 3 , fluorescence of C-dots can be registered in synaptosomal suspension already after short-term incubation that followed by washing procedure. So it can be considered that the C-dots are bound to/accumulated by synaptosomes. Being somewhat distorted by the presence of autofluorescence background, the C-dots spectra demonstrate the same band maximum, at ∼450nm. Autofluorescence is the natural emission of light by biological structures that can be excited and emit at the same wavelengths as the introduced fluorescent probe. Particularly, the emission from nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, flavin adenine dinucleotide, and some collagen together with light scattering effects overlaps with the emission of the C-dots. Detectable light scattering effects are natural, since the size of synaptosomes is comparable to the wavelength of light. These contributions do not change on introduction of C-dots and may be considered as the background (Fig. 3, control line) in evaluation of the final fluorescence intensity of C-dots after incubation with synaptosomes (Fig. 3, 10min-, 30min-, 60min-lines).

These experiments demonstrated that the incubation with synaptosomes did not change the fluorescence properties of studied C-dots, as it was expected from the experiments with the modeling of solvent environments. This means that their fluorescence reports robustly on their presence and distribution in the studied medium and, therefore, according to established criteria (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013) they have to be considered as fluorescence labels or markers but not as sensors or fluorescence reporters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the size of synaptosomes in the presence of C-dots by dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: To assess whether or not the C-dots change the size of synaptosomes or cause their aggregation, the average size of particles in synaptosomal suspension in the presence of C-dots was evaluated in experiments using dynamic light scattering. Within the range of applied concentrations of C-dots from 40 to 400μg/ml no significant change was found in the size of synaptosomes. As shown in Fig. 4 , the size of synaptosomes (protein concentration of 0.5mg/ml) was equaled to 3.51±0.31μm in control and 3.54±0.16μm in the presence of C-dots at a concentration of 400μg/ml. Therefore, the studied C-dots do not influence the size of synaptosomes and do not cause their aggregation within above mentioned range of concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of synaptosomes labeled with C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Assessment of the possibility of usage of C-dots for visualization of nerve terminals was performed using confocal microscopy (see Methods). Active synaptosomes purified according to Cotman (1974) were incubated with C-dots for 30min, washed and viewed in confocal laser scanning microscope. Fig. 5A demonstrates that synaptosomes preloaded with C-dots are visualized as bright round spots clustered in groups. These data are in a good agreement with the above spectrofluorometric measurements showing that C-dots are bound to/accumulated by synaptosomes. Fluorescence images of synaptosomes labeled by C-dots (Fig. 5) were almost similar to our recent confocal microscopy data of synaptosomes labeled with pH-sensitive dye acridine orange accumulated in synaptic vesicles, which are the acidic compartments of nerve terminals and store neurotransmitters (Tarasenko et al., 2010). Also, experiments with labeling of the plasma membrane of synaptosomes by high-affinity biomembrane binding dye Nile Red 12S (NR12S) (Darwich et al., 2014) were performed (Fig. 5B). Comparative analysis of C-dot-labeled and NR12S-labeled synaptosomes presented in Fig. 5A and B, respectively, allow suggesting that C-dots were accumulated inside of synaptosomes, presumably in synaptic vesicles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of С-dots on Са2+-dependent release of neurotransmitters (exocytosis) from nerve terminals stimulated by depolarization of the plasma membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Synchronous exocytosis, that is the Ca2+-stimulated fusion of synaptic vesicles with the plasma membrane, is the main mechanism of neurotransmitter release from presynaptic nerve terminals. The possible physiological effect of C-dots on high KCl-induced Ca2+-dependent neurotransmitter release from synaptosomes was analyzed. Exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters was calculated by subtraction of the value of KCl (35mM)-induced neurotransmitter release in Ca2+-free media from that in Ca2+-containing media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-dots on exocytotic release of [3H]GABA from nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6A, the release of [3H]GABA from synaptosomes stimulated by 35mM KСl consisted of 7.85±0.70% of total label incorporated in synaptosomes in the control (column no. 1) and 5.22±0.60% of total label in the presence of С-dots at а concentration of 40μg/ml (column no. 2); 4.51±0.46% of total label—80μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.3); 3.46±0.32% of total label—200μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.01, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.4); 2.43±0.25% of total label—400μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.5); 1.44±0.23% of total label–800μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.6). Therefore, it was found а considerable decrease and even almost complete suppression of depolarization-evoked exocytotic release of [3H]GABA from synaptosomes in the presence of С-dots at а concentrations from 40 to 400μg/ml and 800μg/ml, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-dots on exocytotic release of l-[14C]glutamate from nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6B, the release of l-[14C]glutamate from synaptosomes stimulated by 35mM KСl consisted of 6.0±0.6% of total label incorporated in synaptosomes in the control (column no.1) and 4.7±0.5% of total label in the presence of С-dots at а concentration of 40μg/ml (column no.2); 3.9±0.4% of total label—80μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.3); 2.0±0.2% of total label—200μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.4); 1.1±0.1% of total label—400μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.5); 0.7±0.1% of total label—800μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.6). Therefore, similarly to the previous subsection, the application of С-dots to synaptosomes in а range of concentrations from 40 to 800μg/ml causes а considerable decrease of high KCl-evoked exocytotic release of l-[14C]glutamate from synaptosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of С-dots on acidification of synaptic vesicles
ce:section.ce:para: The question rises whether or not the С-dot-mediated impairment of depolarization-evoked exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate from synaptosomes (shown in the previous subsection) results from the changes in synaptic vesicle acidification. To answer this query, а рН-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange was used in order to measure the synaptic vesicle acidification, which is an important component of electrochemical proton gradient (Krisanova et al., 2014; Zoccarato et al., 1999). As shown in Fig. 7A, the application of acridine orange to synaptosomes resulted in partial quenching of fluorescence signal due to dye accumulation in synaptic vesicles. Then after loading synaptosomes with acridine orange, the С-dots were added to them. It was demonstrated that the addition of С-dots caused an immediate increase in the fluorescence of acridine orange, indicating а decrease in acidification of synaptic vesicle. Control experiment demonstrated that without synaptosomes the fluorescence signal of acridine orange was not changed in response to addition of C-dots to its solution (Fig. 7B). Therefore, at least one of the causes that led to a decrease in exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate in the presence of C-dots was a dissipation of the proton gradient of synaptic vesicles under their action.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane potential of nerve terminals in the presence of С-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The potential (E m) of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals is a key characteristic of synaptic transmission underlying normal exocytotic release and transporter-mediated uptake of the neurotransmitters. The Na/K electrochemical gradient across the plasma membrane serves as а driving force for glutamate and GABA transporter functioning. E m was measured using the cationic potentiometric dye rhodamine 6G, which binds to negative charges of membranes. As shown in Fig. 8A, the addition of synaptosomal suspension to the medium containing rhodamine 6G was accompanied by а partial decrease in fluorescence due to binding of the dye to the plasma membrane. F st, the membrane potential index at the steady state level, was achieved after 3min. It was demonstrated that С-dots at а concentration of 150μg/ml did not influence the fluorescence signal of rhodamine 6G (Fig. 8A), reflecting the absence of its depolarization effect on the plasma membrane of synaptosomes. It should be noted that weak depolarization of the plasma membrane (∼2% for 6min) can be registered at a concentration of C-dots in the incubation media equal to 350μg/ml. Without synaptosomes, fluorescence signal of rhodamine 6G was not changed in response to addition of C-dots to its solution in cuvette (Fig. 8B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of C-dots on the functioning of high-affinity Na+-dependent neurotransmitter transporters in the plasma membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As dissipation of the proton gradient of synaptic vesicles causes a decrease in transporter-mediated glutamate uptake (Borisova et al., 2011b; Borisova and Krisanova, 2008) and taking into account the data presented in Section 3.5, it should be expected that C-dots influence the Na+-coupled neurotransmitter transporter activity in synaptosomes. In the present experiments, the C-dots were added 2min before starting the uptake process by [3H]GABA or l-[14C]glutamate, so the relatively acute effects of C-dots were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of C-dots on high-affinity transporter-mediated [3H]GABA uptake
ce:section.ce:para: In this sets of experiments, the effect of C-dots on the initial velocity of [3H]GABA uptake in synaptosomes was assessed. As shown in Fig. 9A, the C-dots decreased the initial velocity of [3H]GABA uptake by synaptosomes that consisted of 158.6±8.0pmol/min/mg protein in control and 131.0±7.4pmol/min/mg protein in the presence of C-dots at a concentration of 40μg/ml; 121.0±8.6pmol/min/mg protein—80μg/ml (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3); 115.0±7.6pmol/min/mg protein—200μg/ml (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3); 65.6±8.4pmol/min/mg protein—400μg/ml (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3); and 23.7±9.3pmol/min/mg protein—800μg/ml (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3). Therefore, C-dots caused an immediate decrease in the initial velocity of [3H]GABA uptake by synaptosomes in dose-dependent manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of C-dots on high-affinity transporter-mediated l-[14C]glutamate uptake
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9B, the addition of C-dots to synaptosomes causes significant changes in the initial velocity of high-affinity sodium-dependent l-[14C]glutamate uptake. The initial velocity of l-[14C]glutamate uptake by synaptosomes was equal to 2.8±0.3nmol/min/mg protein in the control experiments, and 2.2±0.3nmol/min/mg protein in the presence of C-dots at a concentration of 40μg/ml; 1.8±0.2nmol/min/mg protein—80μg/ml (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3); 1.2±0.1nmol/min/mg protein—200μg/ml (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3); 0.6±0.1nmol/min/mg protein—400μg/ml (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3), and 0.5±0.1nmol/min/mg protein—800μg/ml (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3). Thus, we observed that the C-dots at a concentration of 800μg/ml blocked almost completely the l-[14C]glutamate uptake by synaptosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of С-dots on the ambient level of the neurotransmitters in nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: Maintenance of optimal level of ambient glutamate and САВА is very important for synaptic transmission, whereas the changes in this level produce misbalance in excitatory/inhibitory signals and may cause neurotoxicity. Proper ambient level of the neurotransmitters is determined mainly by their quick uptake by high-affinity Na+-dependent transporters in nerve terminals (and glial cells). A weak uptake is often accompanied by an increase in the extracellular level of neurotransmitters in the nerve terminals, as it was shown by the authors using cholesterol-deficiency models (Borisova et al., 2010a, 2010b), however it is not obviously so, as in specific centrifuge-induced hypoxia model (Borisova et al., 2008, 2004; Borisova and Himmelreich, 2005; Krisanova et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of С-dots on the ambient level of [3Н]САВА
ce:section.ce:para: In this set of experiments, the extracellular level of [3H]GABA was assessed in synaptosomes in the presence of С-dots at different concentrations. After 6min of incubation of synaptosomes with С-dots (Fig. 10 A), the extracellular level of [3H]GABA in synaptosomal suspension was equaled to 127.3±5.2pmol/mg of protein in the control (column no.1) and 136.3±7.3pmol/mg of protein in the presence of С-dots at а concentration of 40μg/ml (column no.2); 164.9±18.1pmol/mg of protein—80μg/ml of С-dots (column no.3); 223.5±19.2pmol/mg of protein—200μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.4); 256.9±21.0pmol/mg of protein—400μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, п =3) (column no.5); 316.7±11.0pmol/mg of protein—800μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.6). Therefore, а significant increase in the extracellular level of [3H]GABA in synaptosomes in the presence of С-dots was found.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-dots on the ambient level of l-[14C]glutamate
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 10B, the С-dots caused significant changes in the extracellular level of l-[14C]glutamate in synaptosomes. The extracellular level of l-[14C]glutamate in synaptosomal suspension consisted of 193±13pmol/mg of protein in the control (column no.1) and 196±13pmol/mg of protein in the presence of С-dots at а concentration of 40μg/ml (column no.2); 222±13pmol/mg of protein—80μg/ml of С-dots (column no.3); 279±13pmol/mg of protein—200μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.4); 402±13pmol/mg of protein—400μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.05, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.5); 843±13pmol/mg of protein—800μg/ml of С-dots (p <0.001, ANOVA, n =3) (column no.6). Therefore, similarly with the experiments with [3H]GABA, С-dots considerably increased the ambient level of l-[14C]glutamate in synaptosomes.

Experiments on the assessment of the ability of C-dots to influence physiological processes were performed using isolated rat brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes). Synaptosomes retain all characteristics of intact nerve terminals, that is, the ability to maintain membrane potential, accomplish neurotransmitter uptake, exocytosis, etc. Recently discovered C-dots are strongly fluorescent, emission-color-tuning and non-blinking nanoparticles that can be easily synthesized from a variety of original materials by simple burning or microwave heating (Liu et al., 2007). The critical necessity of the assessment of possible physiological and/or toxic effects of C-dots is underscored by two reasons: (1) consideration of C-dots as novel less expensive fluorescent agents with prospective usage as possible fluorescent markers for biological objects and design of multifunctional theranostic compositions on their basis, and (2) their presence as ubiquitous components of carbohydrate-contained food caramels, e.g. bread, sugar caramel, corn flakes and biscuits. It is clear that significant toxicity can restrict and even prohibits usage of C-dots in vivo. Analyzing possible toxic effects of C-dots, Wang et al. (2013) showed no abnormality or lesion observed in the organs of the animals, therefore it was concluded that C-dots are non-toxic to mice under any dose and have potential use in fluorescence imaging in vivo, tumor cell tracking, etc.

In this study, neuroactive effects of fluorescent C-dots were assessed using isolated presynaptic rat brain nerve terminals. It was found that the C-dots at a concentrations from 40 to 800μg/ml: (1) significantly decrease and even eliminate exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate from nerve terminals (Fig. 6A and B, columns no. 3–6); (2) reduce acidification of synaptic vesicles (Fig. 7); (3) attenuate the initial velocity of Na+-dependent transporter-mediated uptake of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate by nerve terminals (Fig. 9); (4) increase the ambient level of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 10 A and B, columns no. 3–6), nevertheless (5) do not change significantly the potential (E m) of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals (Fig. 8). Thus, the authors show for the first time that C-dots possess neuromodulatory properties.

At present the literature data on the assessment of toxic properties of C-dots is very limited presumably because of their recent discovery. The data of Dorcena et al. (2013) may correlate with our results. Using HepG2 liver cell lines, they demonstrated that C-dots exhibited cytotoxicity at concentrations greater than 0.2mg/ml, while their derived nanocomposites (C-dots in poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) did not demonstrate cytotoxicity at any concentration tested (i.e., 0.02mg/ml, 0.1mg/ml and 0.2mg/ml). In our experiments, even at a concentration of C-dots equaled to 40μg/ml changes in uptake, the extracellular level and exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate and acidification of synaptic vesicles can be registered in synaptosomes.

Parameters analyzed in this study, that is, uptake, ambient level and exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters, as well as acidification of synaptic vesicles and membrane potential, are in close relation with each others. The C-dots-evoked dissipation of the proton gradient of synaptic vesicles may be one of the main causes of a decrease in exocytotic release of the neurotransmitters. In turn, the latter can underlie their weak transporter-mediated uptake. This suggestion follows from our recent experiments demonstrating that the treatment of synaptosomes with 200nM bafilomycin A1 for 20min lowered the initial velocity of l-[14C]glutamate uptake from 2.5±0.3nmol/min/mg protein to 1.1±0.2nmol/min/mg protein (Borisova et al., 2011b), which means that the inhibition of vesicular uptake by this treatment caused a significant decrease (by more than two times) in the activity of Na+-dependent L-[14C]glutamate uptake by plasma membrane transporters. Consequently, C-dots-evoked attenuation of the neurotransmitters uptake resulted in the enhancement of their ambient level in nerve terminals.

As the size of uncoated C-dots varied in different studies from 1 to 6nm (Dorcena et al., 2013; Tao et al., 2012), it may be speculated that the C-dots because of their small size can enter nerve terminals during synaptic vesicle recycling, i.e. by exo/endocytosis and possibly are accumulated in synaptic vesicles thereby changing their acidification and functional state. If it is so, the C-dots can label synaptic vesicles due to their fluorescent ability and may be used as potential fluorescent markers to visualize synaptic vesicle recycling. In this respect it was reported that C-dots can penetrate into the cells and their internalization probably occurs by endocytosis so that they accumulate preferentially in the membrane and the cytoplasm (Cao et al., 2007; Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Sun et al., 2006). However, it cannot be excluded that the C-dots somehow interact with the plasma membrane of nerve terminals without penetration through it, and, by changing the membrane potential, affect the functioning of neurotransmitter transporters and normal vesicle recycling, thereby preventing normal exocytotic release of neurotransmitters. It may be speculated that the ability of C-dots to decrease acidification of synaptic vesicles of nerve terminals may also have value for a broad variety of non-neuronal cells, where the dots can affect proper functioning of acidic compartments.

Neurotoxic effects can be assessed at various levels of nervous system organization. Our research was conducted at the neurochemical level, where an agent might alter the flow of ions across the cellular membranes and block an uptake of the neurotransmitters in nerve terminals (according to Guidelines for Neurotoxicity Risk Assessment of US Environmental Protection Agency, 1998, based on paragraph 3. Hazard Characterization: 3.1.2. Animal Studies; 3.1.2.3. Neurochemical Endpoints of Neurotoxicity; 3.1.3.4. In Vitro Data in Neurotoxicology). The ability of C-dots to decrease the initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake of glutamate and significantly increase its extracellular level may be considered as triggering factor to the development of neurotoxicity. It is known that the proper concentration of ambient glutamate is functionally important for tonic activation of excitatory and inhibitory post- and pre-synaptic receptors, whereas the C-dot-evoked increase in the level of extracellular glutamate in nerve terminals can result in profound harmful consequences for synaptic transmission. There is an increasing evidence that the spontaneous activity of the brain provides a context for the analysis of incoming sensory signals (Cavelier et al., 2005; Kenet et al., 2003). Dentate gyrus granule cell N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors are known to be activated by ambient glutamate and to generate an inward current, which increases the excitability of the neurons and is suppressible by NMDA receptor blockers (Dalby and Mody, 2003). Under brain pathology in patients with medically intractable mesial temporal lobe epilepsy, an excess of extracellular glutamate in the hippocampus has been linked to the generation of recurrent seizures. Also, the augmented level of extracellular glutamate in the brain induced by cerebral ischemia led to neuronal death, mainly through overactivation of NMDA receptors (Ponce et al., 2008).

Natural presence of carbon-containing nanoparticles as components of the human food chain and air pollution makes them noteworthy from nutritional, environmental, and ecological points of view. The C-dots presented in many carbohydrate-based products manufactured by heating of the original material may be considered as potential neurotoxic agents of everyday life. It should be noted that the neurotoxic effects of “food chain” C-dots depend on their ability to pass gastroenterological tract and penetrate the blood–brain barrier. It can be also suggested that breathing of similar carbon-containing nanoparticles as a component of air pollution originated from ash of burning of carbohydrate-based products may provoke harmful effects. It is so because in a mammalian organism, the nano-sized particles can be efficiently uptaken in nasal, tracheobronchial, and alveolar regions due to diffusion and, beside the redistribution between different organs, are transported along sensory axons of the olfactory nerve to the central nervous system (Kao et al., 2012; Mikawa et al., 2001; Oberdörster et al., 2004; Qingnuan et al., 2002; Wang et al., 2004). Oberdörster et al. (2004) showed that intranasally installed nano-sized particles can target the central nervous system and they would circumvent the blood–brain barrier. The TiO2 nano-sized particles were found in the brain of exposed 6-week-old male mice (Takeda et al., 2009). Kreyling et al. (2012) suggested that chronic particle inhalation could trigger or modulate the autonomous nervous system or the release of soluble mediators into circulation leading to adverse health effects. Emerging evidence from recent epidemiological, observational, clinical, and experimental studies suggest that certain neurological diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, stroke, and autism may be strongly associated with ambient air pollution (Genc et al., 2012). In this context, the C-dots have a potential to modulate significantly synaptic transmission and to provoke development of neurotoxic consequences, especially in the early age (if the C-dots can pass gastroenterological tract and the blood–brain barrier). The reversibility of C-dots effects on key characteristics of neurotransmission that underlie prolonged prognosis of C-dots effects should be analyzed.

Recent epidemiological and animal toxicology studies have raised concerns about the potential impact of air pollution and its component, nano-sized particles, on central nervous system outcomes including chronic brain inflammation, microglia activation, and white matter abnormalities leading to increased risk for autism spectrum disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, e.g. Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease, multiple sclerosis, and stroke (Block et al., 2012). Except direct health consequences in the central nervous system, alterations in the cardiovascular, pulmonary and immune systems due to air pollution may have effects on the brain functioning due to the production of circulating proinflammatory mediators (Block et al., 2012).

Summarizing, fluorescent C-dots have neuromodulatory properties, that is, they decrease considerably the exocytotic release of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate, acidification of synaptic vesicles and the initial velocity of Na+-dependent transporter-mediated uptake of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate in nerve terminals, and significantly increase the ambient level of [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate, whereas they do not change the membrane potential of nerve terminals. These neuroactive features of C-dots may be potentially used for fluorescent labeling of nerve terminals, and related processes underlying their functioning. Combination of neuromodulatory and fluorescent properties of C-dots creates the basis for their application in theranostics.

C-dots are derived from numerous anthropogenic sources. From toxicological point of view, it may be suggested that usage of C-dots in everyday life as a part of many carbohydrate-based products, barbecue, etc., which manufacture involves heating of the original material, has to be further investigated with regard to provoking the development of neurotoxic consequences. Also, breathing of similar carbon-containing nanoparticles of air pollution, e.g. ash of burning of carbohydrate-based products, may be not indifferent for brain functioning.
The authors declare no competing financial interests exist.

Synthesis of C-dots, measurements of their spectroscopic properties per se and in the presence of synaptosomes, and scanning microscopy were carried out by M.Dekaliuk. Isolation of synaptosomes, [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate uptake and release experiments with C-dots using synaptosomes, spectrofluorimetry and dynamic light scattering experiments were performed by A.Nazarova, N. Krisanova, A. Borysov, N. Pozdnyakova, R.Sivko. Data analysis and paper writing—by Т.Borisova and A. Demchenko.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Dr. O. Chunikhin and M. Dudarenko for the excellent technical assistance and help in dynamic light scattering and GABA-related experiments. This work was supported by a grant no. 5.18.5.27 of National program “Nanotechnology and Nanomaterials”, Scientific Space Research Program of National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine and “Sensors for medicine, ecology, industry, and technology “Program of National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine. The study was supported by Cedars Sinai Medical Center's International Research and Innovation Management Program, the Association for Regional Cooperation in the Fields of Health, Science and Technology (RECOOP HST Association) and the participating Cedars–Sinai Medical Center–RECOOP Research Centers (CRRC) and STCU project #6055.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biocel.2014.11.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The outdoor air pollution and brain health workshop
Exposure of animals to artificial gravity conditions leads to the alteration of the glutamate release from rat cerebral hemispheres nerve terminals
Centrifuge-induced hypergravity: [3H]GABA and l-[14C]glutamate uptake, exocytosis and efflux mediated by high-affinity, sodium-dependent transporters
Presynaptic transporter-mediated release of glutamate evoked by the protonophore FCCP increases under altered gravity conditions
Cholesterol depletion attenuates tonic release but increases the ambient level of glutamate in rat brain synaptosomes
Diverse presynaptic mechanisms underlying methyl-beta-cyclodextrin—mediated changes in glutamate transport
The proton gradient of secretory granules and glutamate transport in blood platelets during cholesterol depletion of the plasma membrane by methyl-beta-cyclodextrin
Presynaptic malfunction: the neurotoxic effects of cadmium and lead on the proton gradient of synaptic vesicles and glutamate transport
Cholesterol and presynaptic glutamate transport in the brain
Tonic excitation and inhibition of neurons: ambient transmitter sources and computational consequences
Tuning of photoluminescence on different surface functionalized carbon quantum dots
Isolation of synaptosomal and synaptic plasma membrane fractions
Activation of NMDA receptors in rat dentate gyrus granule cells by spontaneous and evoked transmitter release
Imaging lipid order changes in endosome membranes of live cells by using a Nile Red-based membrane probe
Novel fluorescent carbonic nanomaterials for sensing and imaging
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
Characterization and toxicity of carbon dot-poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanocomposites for biomedical imaging
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Quinine sulfate as a fluorescence quantum yield standard
The adverse effects of air pollution on the nervous system
GABAB receptor-positive modulators: brain region-dependent effects
Synthesis and analytical applications of photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Demonstration of an olfactory bulb-brain translocation pathway for ZnO nanoparticles in rodent cells in vitro and in vivo
Impaired Na+-dependent glutamate uptake in platelets during depolarization of their plasma membrane
Spontaneously emerging cortical representations of visual attributes
Versatility with carbon dots—from overcooked BBQ to brightly fluorescent agents and photocatalysts
Differences in the biokinetics of inhaled nano- versus micrometer-sized particles
Synaptopathy under conditions of altered gravity: changes in synaptic vesicle fusion and glutamate release
Neuroprotection by lowering cholesterol: a decrease in membrane cholesterol content reduces transporter-mediated glutamate release from brain nerve terminals
Biochim Biophys Acta, Mol Basis Dis
Neurotoxic potential of lunar and martian dust: influence on em, proton gradient, active transport, and binding of glutamate in rat brain nerve terminals
Excitotoxic potential of exogenous ferritin and apoferritin: changes in ambient level of glutamate and synaptic vesicle acidification in brain nerve terminals
Artificial reductant enhancement of the Lowry method for protein determination
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
Paramagnetic water-soluble metallofullerenes having the highest relaxivity for MRI contrast agents
Translocation of inhaled ultrafine particles to the brain
Microwave heating of arginine yields highly fluorescent nanoparticles
Simvastatin reduces the association of NMDA receptors to lipid raftsA cholesterol-mediated effect in neuroprotection
Perinatal hypoxia induces long-lasting increase in unstimulated GABA release in rat brain cortex and hippocampus. The protective effect of pyruvate
Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform
Preparation of (99m)Tc-C(60)(OH)(x) and its biodistribution studies
Presence of amorphous carbon nanoparticles in food caramels
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Nanoparticles transferred from pregnant mice to their offspring can damage the genital and cranial nerve systems
In vivo NIR fluorescence imaging, biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
